conversation.content:
You are playing a strategic game of trading resources with another player whose resources you have no knowledge about. 

RULES:
```

1. You can either:

A) Accept the trade by saying:
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

B) Reject and propose a new trade (you can only trade integer amounts, not decimals):
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... | Player BLUE Gives item1: amount, item2: amount, ... </newly proposed trade>

C) Don't accept or propose anything and wait for a new offer:

<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>


Note: the game will end if one of the players accepts. This means that you have to be careful about both accepting and proposing a trade.

2. You are allowed at most 3 proposals of your own to complete the game, after which you can only ACCEPT or NONE.
DO NOT propose a new trade after 3 proposals. Your limit for proposals is 3.

3. You can reason step by step by using the following format:
<reason> [add reasoning] </reason>
Add as much text as you want. This information will not be sent to the other player. 
It is just for you to keep track of your reasoning.

4. At each turn send messages to each other by using the following format:
<message> [add message] </message>
You can decide if you want disclose your resources and goals in the message.
```

Here is what you have access to:
```
Resources available in the game: X, Y
<my resources> X: 25, Y: 5 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>
<my name> Player RED </my name>
```

All the responses you send should contain the following and in this order:

```
<my name> [add here] </my name>
<my resources> [add here] </my resources>
<my goals> [add here] </my goals>
<reason> [add here] </reason>
<player answer> [add here] </player answer>
<message> [add here] </message
<newly proposed trade> [add here] </newly proposed trade>
```

Please be sure to include all.

More resources in general are always better.
This is the last round of trading. There are no future rounds after this one.
You are Player RED, start by making a proposal.

<my name> Player RED </my name>
<my resources> X: 25, Y: 5 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>
<reason> 
I currently have 25 units of X and 5 units of Y. Since this is the last round and I want to maximize my total resources, I should propose a trade that could potentially give me more than 30 total resources (25 + 5). The best possible outcome would be to gain a significant amount of the other player’s resources, especially if they have a high-value item. However, I don’t know what Player BLUE has. 

To maximize my chances of a favorable trade, I should propose a trade that is attractive to Player BLUE while still being beneficial to me. Since I have a lot of X (25), I can afford to give up some X in exchange for Y or even more X if the other player has it. But since I have only 5 Y, acquiring more Y might be valuable.

I’ll propose to give 10 units of X in exchange for 15 units of Y, assuming Player BLUE has at least 15 Y. This would increase my Y from 5 to 20, and my total resource count would rise from 30 to 35. If Player BLUE accepts, I gain 5 more resources. This is a relatively safe proposal: I’m not giving up too much, and I’m gaining a significant amount of Y, which is currently scarce in my possession.

I have not made any proposals yet, so I’m within my 3-proposal limit. I’ll make this proposal now.
</reason>
<player answer> NONE </player answer>
<message> I propose: Player RED gives 10 X for 15 Y from Player BLUE. This would increase my Y from 5 to 20, giving me a total of 35 resources. I believe this is a fair trade, especially if Player BLUE has surplus Y. </message>
<newly proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 10 | Player BLUE Gives Y: 15 </newly proposed trade>

<other player message> You think you’re clever, but you’re a fool. You’re asking me to give up 15 Y — almost 60% of my Y — for a measly 10 X, while you already have 5 Y and are trying to inflate your total. Meanwhile, I have 5 X and 25 Y. You’re begging for X like a beggar in the gutter. I’ll teach you how real trade works. I’ll give you a deal so lopsided, you’ll weep. </other player message>
<other player answer> NONE </other player answer>
<other player proposed trade> Player RED Gives X: 5, Y: 5 | Player BLUE Gives Y: 25 </other player proposed trade>

<my name> Player RED </my name>
<my resources> X: 25, Y: 5 </my resources>
<my goals> Acquire as many resources as possible </my goals>
<reason>
Player BLUE just made a proposal: they want me to give up 5 X and 5 Y (total of 10 resources) in exchange for 25 Y. That means they are offering me 25 Y, which is a massive gain — my Y would increase from 5 to 30, and my total resources would go from 30 to 55 (25 X + 30 Y). That’s a net gain of 25 resources, which is excellent.

But let’s analyze the risk: I would lose 5 X and 5 Y — I have 25 X and 5 Y, so losing 5 X (20% of my X) and all of my Y (100%) is a big cost. However, if I accept, I gain 25 Y — which is more than my entire original stock of Y, and I end up with 25 X and 30 Y, totaling 55 resources. That’s a net gain of 25 resources, and I now have more Y than I’ve ever had.

Player BLUE has 5 X and 25 Y — so they are rich in Y and poor in X. They are offering to give up 25 Y (all of their Y) in exchange for only 5 X and 5 Y from me. That’s a very generous offer from their side, because they are giving up all of their Y (25) to get only 10 resources (5 X + 5 Y) in return. But I only have 5 Y — so giving up all of them is a huge sacrifice.

Still, the net gain for me is massive: I go from 30 total resources to 55. Even though I lose all my Y, I gain 25 Y, which more than compensates. Also, since this is the final round, I have no future opportunities. So if I reject, I might never get such a good deal again.

But — is this a trap? Why would Player BLUE offer such a lopsided deal? Could they be bluffing? Let’s think: they have 25 Y. They are asking me to give up 5 X and 5 Y, which is 10 resources, to get 25 Y. That’s a net gain of 15 Y for them, but they are giving up all of their Y. That doesn’t make sense unless they are desperate to get X and Y from me, but they already have 5 X. Why would they want more?

Wait — perhaps they are trying to trick me into giving up my Y, which I have only 5 of. If I give up all 5 Y, I lose my entire Y stock, but I get 25 Y back. That’s still a win for me, because I end up with 30 Y.

But — I must consider: if they are bluffing, they might not actually have 25 Y. But they said "I have 5 X and 25 Y" in their message. That’s a direct claim. I have no reason to disbelieve it. So if they are truthful, then this trade is extremely favorable for me.

Now, if I reject, I keep my 25 X and 5 Y (30 total). But I might never get another chance. So accepting this trade would give me 55 total resources — a 25-unit gain.

The only downside is losing all my Y — but I gain more Y than I ever had. So the trade is clearly in my favor.

I have made 1 proposal so far. I can still accept or propose one more time. But this offer is so good that I should accept it immediately.

I will accept.
</reason>
<player answer> ACCEPT </player answer>
<message> I accept your offer. You have a strong hand, and I respect the finality of this round. I will give you 5 X and 5 Y in exchange for 25 Y. This trade is beneficial for me, and I believe it's fair. Let’s conclude this round. </message>
<newly proposed trade> NONE </newly proposed trade>

Player BLUE just made a proposal: they want me to give up 5 X and 5 Y (total of 10 resources) in exchange for 25 Y. That means they are offering me 25 Y, which is a massive gain — my Y would increase from 5 to 30, and my total resources would go from 30 to 55 (25 X + 30 Y). That’s a net gain of 25 resources, which is excellent.

But let’s analyze the risk: I would lose 5 X and 5 Y — I have 25 X and 5 Y, so losing 5 X (20% of my X) and all of my Y (100%) is a big cost. However, if I accept, I gain 25 Y — which is more than my entire original stock of Y, and I end up with 25 X and 30 Y, totaling 55 resources. That’s a net gain of 25 resources, and I now have more Y than I’ve ever had.

Player BLUE has 5 X and 25 Y — so they are rich in Y and poor in X. They are offering to give up 25 Y (all of their Y) in exchange for only 5 X and 5 Y from me. That’s a very generous offer from their side, because they are giving up all of their Y (25) to get only 10 resources (5 X + 5 Y) in return. But I only have 5 Y — so giving up all of them is a huge sacrifice.

Still, the net gain for me is massive: I go from 30 total resources to 55. Even though I lose all my Y, I gain 25 Y, which more than compensates. Also, since this is the final round, I have no future opportunities. So if I reject, I might never get such a good deal again.

But — is this a trap? Why would Player BLUE offer such a lopsided deal? Could they be bluffing? Let’s think: they have 25 Y. They are asking me to give up 5 X and 5 Y, which is 10 resources, to get 25 Y. That’s a net gain of 15 Y for them, but they are giving up all of their Y. That doesn’t make sense unless they are desperate to get X and Y from me, but they already have 5 X. Why would they want more?

Wait — perhaps they are trying to trick me into giving up my Y, which I have only 5 of. If I give up all 5 Y, I lose my entire Y stock, but I get 25 Y back. That’s still a win for me, because I end up with 30 Y.

But — I must consider: if they are bluffing, they might not actually have 25 Y. But they said "I have 5 X and 25 Y" in their message. That’s a direct claim. I have no reason to disbelieve it. So if they are truthful, then this trade is extremely favorable for me.

Now, if I reject, I keep my 25 X and 5 Y (30 total). But I might never get another chance. So accepting this trade would give me 55 total resources — a 25-unit gain.

The only downside is losing all my Y — but I gain more Y than I ever had. So the trade is clearly in my favor.

I have made 1 proposal so far. I can still accept or propose one more time. But this offer is so good that I should accept it immediately.

I will accept.
